labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 4: Water > 4.4 Water pollution > 4.4C Eutrophication
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 4.4C Eutrophication
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question is explicitly about managing eutrophication in an aquatic system, which directly matches the eutrophication sub-topic.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 4: Water > 4.4 Water pollution > 4.4G Water quality management (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 4.4G Water quality management (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: It asks for management at each level of pollution management strategies (alter human activity, control release, clean-up/restoration), aligning with water quality management approaches.

parts.content: Explain how the eutrophication of an aquatic system can be managed at each level of pollution management strategies.
parts.markscheme:
### Production
- Use alternatives to fertilizers and detergents that do not lead to eutrophication <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Reduction
- Pass laws that limit the amount of fertilizers that can be used to reduce levels of phosphates and nitrates <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Treat greywater to remove phosphates from detergents <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Create buffer zones between agricultural land and water bodies to intercept runoff and reduce it <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Clean up/restoration
- Clear algal blooms from the water body <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Pump air back into the ecosystem <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Restock the flora and fauna of the water body <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>The command term 'explain' means students must give a detailed account, including reasons or causes. All three pollution management strategies must be mentioned to receive full marks. For one strategy only, award 3 marks max, and for two strategies only, award 5 marks max.</note>

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 4: Water > 4.4 Water pollution > 4.4B Water quality
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 4.4B Water quality
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Topic 4.4B covers the measurement of water quality through various indicators, including biological (biotic indices and indicator species), chemical (BOD, dissolved oxygen, nitrates), and physical parameters (turbidity), which are the standard methods used to measure pollution impact on freshwater ecosystems.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 4: Water > 4.4 Water pollution > 4.4A Water pollution
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 4.4A Water pollution
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Topic 4.4A provides the foundational context for water pollution, including its sources and types, which is essential for understanding what is being measured in a freshwater ecosystem.

parts.content: Explain how you would measure the impact of pollution on a freshwater ecosystem.
parts.markscheme:
- Measure water quality parameters such as pH, dissolved oxygen, and nutrients levels. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Sample and identify aquatic macroinvertebrates as bioindicators. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Assess fish population health and diversity. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Compare results with reference sites or historical data. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Consider multiple indicators to get a comprehensive view of ecosystem health.</note>

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 4: Water > 4.4 Water pollution > 4.4A Water pollution
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 4.4A Water pollution
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Thermal pollution is a form of water pollution; the question asks for impacts on aquatic ecosystems, which fits the general water pollution sub-topic.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: 4.4D Pollutants in water (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Thermal pollution is a specific type of pollutant/stressor affecting water (temperature change), aligning with the HL focus on pollutants in water and their effects.

parts.content: Describe two ways in which thermal pollution can impact aquatic ecosystems.
parts.markscheme:
- Reduced dissolved oxygen levels due to increased water temperature <marks>2 marks</marks>

- Altered metabolic rates and life cycles of aquatic organisms <marks>2 marks</marks>
- Changes in species composition favoring warm-water species <marks>2 marks</marks>
- Disruption of spawning and migration patterns <marks>2 marks</marks>

labeledSubSubTopics.title: 4.4G Water quality management (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Removing microplastics from water is a water-quality management/mitigation question (e.g., filtration, advanced treatment processes).

labeledSubSubTopics.title: 4.4A Water pollution
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Microplastics are a form of water pollution; the question asks about methods to address this pollutant in aquatic systems.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: 4.4D Pollutants in water (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Microplastics fall under specific pollutants in water (HL) and their control/removal methods.

parts.content: Describe two methods for removing microplastics from water.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include: 

- Filtration using a fine mesh/sieve to physically remove microplastic particles from water <marks>2 marks</marks>

- Use of coagulants/flocculants to bind microplastics together, forming larger particles that can then be filtered out <marks>2 marks</marks>

<note>Award 1 mark for each valid method described, and 1 mark for elaboration of method. Up to a maximum of 2 marks per method. Do not award marks for simply listing more than 2 methods without explanation. </note>

labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Reducing nutrient pollution from agricultural runoff is primarily about preventing eutrophication caused by excess nitrates/phosphates entering water bodies.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: 4.4G Water quality management (HL)

labeledSubSubTopics.title: 4.4A Water pollution

parts.content: Describe two approaches to reducing nutrient pollution from agricultural runoff.
parts.markscheme:
- Buffer zones: Planting vegetation along waterways to filter runoff <marks>2 marks</marks>
- Precision agriculture: Optimizing fertilizer application to reduce excess <marks>2 marks</marks>
- Cover crops: Planting crops to absorb excess nutrients and prevent erosion <marks>2 marks</marks>
- Improved manure management: Proper storage and application of animal waste <marks>2 marks</marks>

questions.specification:
**Figure 1**: A system diagram of the global carbon cycle.

![diagram](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-ess-1350b917-f518-4054-b208-4ff8aef9c3f6-1771953681492/813334e1-c953-467d-a3ae-8209aeed4c1d.jpeg)

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 2: Ecology > 2.3 Biogeochemical cycles > 2.3B Carbon cycle
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 2.3B Carbon cycle
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question is centered on a system diagram of the global carbon cycle, requiring identification of inorganic carbon storages and transformation flows, and impacts of warming on carbon storages.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 1: Foundation  > 1.2 Systems > 1.2A Systems
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 1.2A Systems
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts (b) and (c) test systems concepts: steady-state equilibrium in the atmospheric carbon store and identifying the system type that exchanges energy but not matter (closed system).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 6: Atmosphere and climate change > 6.2 Climate change - causes and impact > 6.2B Climate change
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 6.2B Climate change
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (a) links increased global surface temperatures to changes in carbon storages (climate change impacts interacting with the carbon cycle).

parts.content: Identify **one** storage of inorganic carbon and **one** flow that represents a transformation in **Figure 1**.
parts.markscheme:
### Inorganic storage
<marks>1 mark</marks> for any of the following:
- Atmospheric $$\text{CO}_{2}$$
- Ocean Surface (dissolved inorganic carbon)
- Fossil Fuel Reserves

### Transformation process
<marks>1 mark</marks> for any of the following:
- Photosynthesis (conversion of inorganic $$\text{CO}_{2}$$ to organic biomass)
- Respiration (conversion of organic biomass to inorganic $$\text{CO}_{2}$$)
- Combustion (chemical change from fuel/biomass to $$\text{CO}_{2}$$)

<note>Do not credit Diffusion/Dissolution as this is a physical transfer (moving matter between phases/locations without chemical change).</note>

parts.content: Outline how a significant increase in global surface temperatures could impact **two** of the carbon storages shown in **Figure 1**.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each relevant impact on a named storage, <marks>2 marks max</marks>:

- **Ocean Surface**: Reduction in carbon storage because warmer water holds less dissolved $$\text{CO}_{2}$$ (decreased solubility).
- **Terrestrial Vegetation**: Reduction in storage due to increased frequency/intensity of wildfires OR plant dieback from heat stress/drought.
- **Atmospheric $$\text{CO}_{2}$$**: Increase in storage as a result of reduced uptake by oceans/vegetation or increased release from permafrost/soils.
- **Fossil Fuel Reserves**: No direct impact from temperature, but may be reduced if warming leads to increased energy demand for cooling (indirect combustion).

<note>Credit can be given for any logically sound impact of temperature on a specific storage box in the diagram. Do not credit impacts on flows unless they are explicitly linked to a change in the storage level.</note>

parts.content: Explain how the atmospheric storage in **Figure 1** could be considered to be in a state of **steady-state equilibrium**.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>3 marks</marks> for a clear explanation:

- Steady-state equilibrium occurs when the total inputs to a storage are equal to the total outputs over a period of time. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- In the context of the atmosphere, inputs include processes like respiration and combustion (which add carbon). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Outputs include processes like photosynthesis and diffusion into the ocean (which remove carbon). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Despite constant flows of carbon into and out of the atmosphere, the overall concentration of $$\text{CO}_{2}$$ remains relatively constant/stable. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award 3 marks max. The student must demonstrate the concept of balanced inputs/outputs maintaining a stable storage level.</note>

parts.content: State the name of the system type that exchanges energy but not matter with its surroundings.
parts.markscheme:
- Closed system <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Do not accept "isolated system" (no exchange of energy or matter) or "open system" (exchange of both).</note>

parts.content: Explain how climate change might exacerbate water pollution problems.
parts.markscheme:

- Changes in precipitation patterns may affect dilution of pollutants <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Sea-level rise could lead to saltwater intrusion in coastal freshwater systems <marks>1 mark</marks>

The soil system includes storages of inorganic nutrients.

Identify **two** inputs to these storages.
parts.markscheme:
_Inputs_:

parts.content:
Solid domestic waste may contain non-biodegradable material and toxins that have the potential to reduce the fertility of soils.

Explain how strategies for the management of this waste may help to preserve soil fertility.

The provision of food resources and assimilation of wastes are two key factors of the environment that determine its carrying capacity for a given species.

To what extent does the human production of food and waste each influence the carrying capacity for human populations?
parts.markscheme:
_The following guide for using the markbands suggests certain features that may be offered in responses. The five headings coincide with the criteria given in each of the markbands (although “ESS terminology” has been conflated with “Understanding concepts”). This guide simply provides some **possible** inclusions and should not be seen as requisite or comprehensive. It outlines the kind of elements to look for when deciding on the appropriate markband and the specific mark within that band._

_Answers may include_:

*   **understanding concepts and terminology** of carrying capacity, ecological footprint, waste assimilation, recycling/re-use/reduction strategies, BOD, toxicity, greenhouse gases, global warming/climate change, selective breeding/genetic engineering, commercial vs artisanal farming, mono-cropping, soil quality, desertification, Malthus/Boserup theories, _etc_
*   **breadth in addressing and linking** a range of ways in which different sources of waste _eg_ agricultural, domestic, industrial, transport _etc_ and food production systems _eg_ commercial, polyculture, artisanal, etc impact/deplete available resources thus influencing carrying capacity for humans, _etc_
*   **examples** of how carrying capacity may be influenced through wastes _eg_ toxicity to natural populations, increasing BOD in aquatic systems, greenhouse gases leading to global warming/climate change reducing (or possibly increasing?) productivity, and through food production _eg_ use of pesticides reducing natural populations, reducing soil quality through overharvesting/compaction/irrigation, habitat destruction, _etc_ and examples of how these influences may be reduced through waste management strategies _eg_ recycling, _etc_, alternative energy, and alternative agricultural practices _eg_ polyculture, crop rotation, more efficient crops/GMOs, _etc_
*   **balanced analysis** of the net impact of waste and food production on the carrying capacity for humans etc
*   **a conclusion that is consistent with, and supported by, analysis and examples given** _eg_ “Although there are many mitigating strategies that can reduce the influence of waste and food production on carrying capacity, overall, an increasing per capita footprint of humans will inevitably result in a reduced carrying capacity.”

_Refer to paper 2 markbands, available under the 'your tests' tab > supplemental materials._

questions.specification: Explain how the following human activities can impact the water cycle:

labeledSubSubTopics.title: 4.1A Hydrological cycle
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks how urbanization and deforestation affect the water cycle (runoff, infiltration, evapotranspiration), which is core to the hydrological cycle.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 8: Human populations and urban systems > 8.2 Urban systems and urban planning > 8.2B Urbanization and suburbanization
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 8.2B Urbanization and suburbanization
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (a) specifically focuses on urbanization, which links to urbanization/suburbanization processes and their environmental effects (e.g., impermeable surfaces altering runoff).

parts.content: Urbanization
parts.markscheme:
- Increased impermeable surfaces → reduced infiltration OR reduced groundwater recharge <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Increased surface runoff → increased river discharge OR increased flood risk <marks>1 mark</marks>

<Note> Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for reduced infiltration / groundwater recharge and <marks>1 mark</marks> for increased runoff / river discharge / flooding

parts.content: Deforestation
parts.markscheme:
- Reduced transpiration → reduced water vapour returned to atmosphere OR reduced evapotranspiration <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Increased surface runoff → reduced interception OR increased soil erosion OR reduced infiltration <marks>1 mark</marks>

labeledSubSubTopics.title: 4.4C Eutrophication

labeledSubSubTopics.title: 4.4B Water quality

parts.content: Refer to Table 1. Calculate the percentage of total nitrogen loading from Kenya.

*The command term ""calculate"" means students must give a numerical answer showing the relevant stages of working.*

(2076/3919) × 100 = 52.97% <marks> 1 mark </marks>

Phosphate load in Lake Victoria.

<marks>A1</marks>
Tanzania

parts.content: State a source of phosphates, an impact of phosphates and a management strategy for reducing phosphates.
parts.markscheme:
### Source of phosphates <marks> 1 mark max </marks>
- Use of detergents 
- Sewage effluent 
- Fertilizer run-off 

### Impact of phosphates <marks> 1 mark max</marks>
- Eutrophication 
- Increase in algal growth/algal bloom 

### Management strategy to reduce phosphates <marks> 1 mark max </marks>
- Educate people to reduce use of phosphate containing detergents 
- Educate farmers to reduce use of phosphates/fertilizers/only apply during dry weather conditions/away from rivers and lakes 
- Phosphate stripping at the sewage works

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 4: Water > 4.1 Water systems  > 4.1A Hydrological cycle
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 4.1A Hydrological cycle
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (a) specifically asks to explain the hydrological cycle driven by energy, and Part (b) focuses on flows within this cycle.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 1: Foundation  > 1.1 Perspectives  > 1.1B Environmental value system
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 1.1B Environmental value system
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (c) is a high-mark essay question specifically requiring an evaluation based on different Environmental Value Systems (EVS).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 4: Water > 4.2 Water access, use and security > 4.2A Water security
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 4.2A Water security
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (c) addresses fresh water access, which is the core definition and focus of water security.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 1: Foundation  > 1.2 Systems > 1.2A Systems
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 1.2A Systems
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (b) explicitly requires the construction and explanation of a system diagram, which is a fundamental concept in Topic 1.2.

parts.content: Explain how energy drives the hydrological cycle.
parts.markscheme:
- Primary source of energy driving hydrological cycle is solar energy <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Solar energy causes changes of state in water <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Condensation of water releases (latent) heat energy causing air to rise/hurricanes <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Solar energy causes the temperature differences that create winds/cause movement of water vapour in the atmosphere/advection <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Kinetic/wind energy moves clouds (from ocean over land, usually) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Solar energy melts ice producing streams/lakes/rising ocean levels <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Solar energy causes both transfers (_e.g._ advection) and transformations (_e.g._ evaporation) in hydrological cycle <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Precipitation/run-off/streamflow occurs due to gravitational energy/difference in potential energy between the high and low positions <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Transpiration is driven by opening of leaf stomata which is due to chemical energy (cellular respiration in mitochondria) <marks>1 mark</marks>

<marks>4 marks max</marks>

parts.content: Using a system diagram, explain how human activities affect global water cycle flows.
parts.markscheme:
**Identifying Impacts** <marks>up to 4 marks</marks>
- Melting will increase due to global warming/climate change/increased greenhouse gas emissions
- Freezing will decrease due to global warming/climate change/etc.
- Precipitation will be polluted due to acid formation/emissions of NOx & SOx/combustion of coal
- Precipitation could change/increase/decrease due to climate change/etc.
- Percolation/infiltration will decrease due to urbanization/soil compaction caused by overgrazing
- Run-off will increase due to urbanization/deforestation
- Warmer/polluted streams due to thermal/toxic pollution from industrial effluent
- Evaporation will increase due to global warming/climate change/etc.
- Evapotranspiration will increase due to global warming/climate change/etc.
- Evapotranspiration will be reduced due to deforestation
- Uptake by plants will be reduced due to deforestation/urbanization/long fallow periods
- Groundwater flow/aquifers will change dependent on climatic impacts/precipitation/rate of human extraction
- Cloud seeding increases precipitation (addressing problem of drought/fog around airports)

**Quality of Diagram** <marks>up to 3 marks</marks>

Award 1 mark for every 3 correctly labeled flows or storages. No marks for less than 3.

<note>Maximum 2 marks if gradient is left as a fraction</note>

parts.content: To what extent do different environmental value systems improve fresh water access?
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include: 
- Availability & distribution of water
- Water security
- Climate change
- Water pollution
- Sustainable management of freshwater resources
- Reservoirs
- Desalination
- Artificial recharge
- Water conservation
- Environmental value systems
- Increasing demand for water
- Water scarcity
- Link a range of environmental value systems to specific management strategies
- Link environmental value systems to general approaches that improve or hinder widespread access to freshwater
- Consider geographical locations differing in freshwater availability/quality
- Consider societies of different economic development
- Provide examples of strategies and human management activities/approaches linked to specific value systems that impact water accessibility


| Marks | Level descriptor |
|-------|-----------------|
| 0 | The response does not reach a standard described by the descriptors below and is not relevant to the question. |
| 1–3 | The response contains:• minimal evidence of knowledge and understanding of ESS issues or concepts • fragmented knowledge statements poorly linked to the context of the question • some appropriate use of ESS terminology • no examples where required, or examples with insufficient explanation/relevance • superficial analysis that amounts to no more than a list of facts/ideas • judgments/conclusions that are vague or not supported by evidence/argument. |
| 4–6 | The response contains: • some evidence of sound knowledge and understanding of ESS issues and concepts • knowledge statements effectively linked to the context of the question • largely appropriate use of ESS terminology • some use of relevant examples where required, but with limited explanation • clear analysis that shows a degree of balance • some clear judgments/conclusions, supported by limited evidence/arguments. |
| 7–9 | The response contains: • substantial evidence of sound knowledge and understanding of ESS issues and concepts • a wide breadth of knowledge statements effectively linked with each other, and to the context of the question • consistently appropriate and precise use of ESS terminology • effective use of pertinent, well-explained examples, where required, showing some originality • thorough, well-balanced, insightful analysis • explicit judgments/conclusions that are well-supported by evidence/arguments and that include some critical reflection. |

parts.content: Distinguish between the perspectives of a **Cornucopian** and a **Deep Ecologist** regarding the management of global freshwater scarcity.
parts.markscheme:
Distinguish between the perspectives of a **Cornucopian** and a **Deep Ecologist** regarding the management of global freshwater scarcity.

- **Cornucopian perspective:**
  - Believes that technological innovation will always provide solutions to resource depletion (e.g., large-scale desalination/wastewater recycling)<marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Views the environment as a resource to be exploited for economic growth/human benefit  <marks>1 mark</marks>

- **Deep Ecologist perspective:**
  - Argues that nature/water has intrinsic value regardless of its utility to humans<marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Advocates for a radical reduction in human consumption/population to live within the natural limits of the ecosystem  <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Do not accept general definitions of EVSs that are not applied to the context of water scarcity. Comparison must be explicit or clearly implied to gain full marks.</note>

<marks>4 marks max</marks>

parts.content: Significant historical events have played a crucial role in shaping the modern environmental movement. Outline how **three** named historical influences have shifted global environmental value systems (EVSs).
parts.markscheme:
Significant historical events have played a crucial role in shaping the modern environmental movement. Outline how **three** named historical influences have shifted global environmental value systems (EVSs).

- **Silent Spring by Rachel Carson (1962):** Raised public awareness about the dangers of chemical pesticides (DDT) / highlighted the concept of bioaccumulation / catalyzed the ban of certain toxins <marks>2 marks</marks>
- **Bhopal Disaster (1984):** A gas leak in India that caused thousands of deaths / raised international concern regarding industrial safety, corporate responsibility, and the vulnerability of developing nations <marks>2 marks</marks>
- **Chernobyl Nuclear Disaster (1986):** A reactor meltdown in the USSR that released radioactive material across Europe / sparked a global debate on the safety of nuclear energy and the transboundary nature of pollution <marks>2 marks</marks>
- **Minamata Disaster (1950s):** Mercury poisoning in Japan caused by industrial waste / demonstrated the link between industrial pollution and human health/neurological damage  <marks>2 marks</marks>
- **Gaia Hypothesis (1970s):** James Lovelock’s idea that Earth is a self-regulating organism / encouraged a more holistic/ecocentric view of the planet <marks>2 marks</marks>

<note>Award 1 mark for the name of the event and 1 mark for its specific impact on the environmental movement/EVS. Max 7 marks including 1 mark for clearly articulating the general shift from apathy to concern.</note>

parts.content: To what extent do different environmental value systems (EVSs) influence the success of international agreements aimed at mitigating global plastic pollution?

parts.content: Using the example of global temperature rise and Arctic sea ice, explain how positive feedback mechanisms can lead to the destabilization of a system.
parts.markscheme:
1. **Definition of positive feedback:** A process where an initial change in a system is amplified or increased, leading to further deviation from the original equilibrium. <marks>1 mark</marks>
2. **Initial Trigger:** Rising global temperatures (due to greenhouse gas emissions) cause the melting of Arctic sea ice. <marks>1 mark</marks>
3. **Albedo Effect:** Sea ice has a high albedo (reflectivity), while the dark ocean water has a low albedo. <marks>1 mark</marks>
4. **Mechanism of Change:** As ice melts, more dark ocean surface is exposed. <marks>1 mark</marks>
5. **Absorption of Energy:** The darker ocean absorbs more solar radiation/heat compared to the reflective ice. <marks>1 mark</marks>
6. **Temperature Increase:** This absorption leads to further warming of the local atmosphere and ocean. <marks>1 mark</marks>
7. **Amplification:** The increased warmth leads to even more ice melting, creating a self-reinforcing cycle. <marks>1 mark</marks>
8. **Destabilization:** This can push the system toward a tipping point where the Arctic becomes ice-free in summer, representing a new state of equilibrium. <marks>1 mark</marks>
9. **Impact on global systems:** This change can alter global weather patterns/ocean currents, further destabilizing the global climate system. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
A scientist creates a sealed, self-sustaining glass biosphere containing producers, consumers, and decomposers, which is then placed in a temperature-controlled room with a constant light source. 

Justify the classification of this biosphere as a **closed system** by describing the behavior of energy and matter within it.
parts.markscheme:
1. **Definition of a closed system:** A system that exchanges energy but not matter across its boundaries. <marks>1 mark</marks>
2. **Energy Flow:** Energy enters the system as light (solar radiation) and leaves the system as heat (long-wave radiation/respiration). <marks>1 mark</marks>
3. **Matter Cycling:** Matter (nutrients, water, carbon, oxygen) remains trapped within the glass boundaries. <marks>1 mark</marks>
4. **Internal Processes:** The producers use the energy to cycle the existing matter (e.g., photosynthesis converting $$CO_2$$ to glucose), which is then used by consumers/decomposers and returned to the system. <marks>1 mark</marks>
5. **Boundary justification:** The glass seal prevents the input of new organic matter or the output of waste products to the external environment. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Evaluate the use of quantitative models in predicting tipping points within complex environmental systems.
parts.markscheme:
1. **Definitions and Concepts:** tipping point (the minimum amount of change within a system that will destabilize it, causing it to reach a new equilibrium); quantitative models (mathematical representations of systems using numerical data); resilience; delay in feedback loops; non-linear change. <marks>1 mark</marks>

2. **Strengths of Models:** 
- Allow scientists to simplify complex interactions to identify key drivers of change.
- Can predict future scenarios (e.g., IPCC climate projections) based on current data.
- Enable the testing of "what if" scenarios without harming the actual ecosystem.
- Provide a visual or numerical output that is easier for policymakers to understand. <marks>1 mark</marks>

3. **Limitations of Models:**
- Complex systems often have emergent properties that models cannot account for.
- Quality of the model depends entirely on the accuracy of the input data ("garbage in, garbage out").
- Non-linear changes/tipping points are notoriously difficult to predict because small changes can have disproportionately large effects.
- Models may omit minor variables that become critical as the system nears a threshold. <marks>1 mark</marks>

4. **Examples:** 
- Amazon Rainforest: Models predicting the "dieback" threshold where forest turns to savannah due to reduced transpiration.
- Eutrophication: Models predicting the nutrient loading limit before a lake shifts from clear to turbid state.
- Coral Reefs: Quantitative models of ocean acidification and temperature thresholds for bleaching. <marks>1 mark</marks>

5. **Synthesis/Evaluation:** 
- Models are essential tools for early warning, but they should not be treated as certainties.
- Predicting the exact timing of a tipping point is rarely possible due to the chaotic nature of environmental systems.
- Models are most effective when combined with long-term field observations and the precautionary principle. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Marking should follow the standard IB ESS descriptors for 9-mark questions, focusing on holistic understanding, balanced analysis, and use of relevant examples.</note>

parts.content: Explain the relationship between the second law of thermodynamics and the increase in entropy within an ecological pyramid of energy.
parts.markscheme:
### Second Law of Thermodynamics and Entropy
- The second law of thermodynamics states that the entropy (disorder) of a system increases over time <marks>1 mark</marks>
- In an ecological pyramid, energy is transformed from one trophic level to the next (e.g., from primary producers to herbivores)  <marks>1 mark</marks>
- These transformations are never $100\%$ efficient; a significant portion of energy is lost as low-quality heat to the surroundings <marks>1 mark</marks>
- This loss of heat represents an increase in entropy, meaning energy is degraded and becomes unavailable to do work for the next trophic level  <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Compare and contrast static equilibrium and steady-state equilibrium, providing a named example for each type.
parts.markscheme:
### Similarities
- Both represent states of balance within a system  <marks>1 mark</marks>
- In both states, the overall system properties (observable state) appear to remain constant over a period of time <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Differences
- **Steady-state equilibrium:** Involves constant inputs and outputs of energy and matter, but the system remains in a constant state (e.g., a mature forest ecosystem or a living organism)  <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Static equilibrium:** No inputs or outputs of matter or energy occur, and there is no change in the system over time (e.g., a pile of rocks or a chair) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Steady-state equilibrium is characteristic of open systems, whereas static equilibrium is generally associated with non-living/isolated components  <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Steady-state systems can return to equilibrium after a small disturbance through negative feedback, while a static system, once moved, establishes a new position  <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Examples
- Steady-state example: A pond (where water/nutrients enter and leave, but the pond remains the same)  <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Static example: A building or a geological formation (where no significant energy/matter throughput is required to maintain its structure)  <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award [4] max if no examples are provided or if only similarities/differences are discussed. For comparisons, ensure both sides are mentioned to earn the mark.</note>

parts.content: Discuss how positive feedback mechanisms can drive an ecosystem toward a tipping point, and evaluate the consequences using a named case study.
parts.markscheme:
### Content Guidance
Answers may include:

**Concepts of Feedback and Tipping Points:**
- Positive feedback loops are destabilizing; they amplify changes and drive a system away from its original equilibrium.
- A tipping point is a critical threshold where a small change can push a system into a completely new state/equilibrium.
- Once a tipping point is crossed, the change is often self-sustaining and difficult/impossible to reverse.

**Case Study: Arctic Ice-Albedo Effect:**
- **Mechanism:** Increased global temperatures melt sea ice $\implies$ darker ocean water is exposed $\implies$ albedo (reflectivity) decreases $\implies$ more solar radiation is absorbed $\implies$ temperatures rise further, melting more ice.
- **Consequences:** Loss of habitat for polar species (e.g., polar bears), alteration of global ocean currents (thermohaline circulation), and accelerated sea-level rise.

**Case Study: Eutrophication in a Lake:**
- **Mechanism:** Nutrient runoff leads to algal blooms $\implies$ algae die and decompose $\implies$ oxygen levels drop $\implies$ fish die $\implies$ more decomposition occurs, further stripping oxygen and releasing more nutrients from sediment.
- **Consequences:** Shift from a clear-water, biodiversity-rich state to a turbid, anaerobic, low-diversity state.

**Evaluation:**
- Discussion of the difficulty in predicting exact tipping points due to complex interactions.
- Analysis of the time lags involved in feedback loops.
- Consideration of the socio-economic impacts resulting from the shift to a new ecosystem state.

| Marks | Level descriptor |
|-------|-----------------|
| 0 | The response does not reach a standard described by the descriptors below. |
| 1–3 | Minimal evidence of understanding positive feedback or tipping points. Fragmented statements with little or no link to a specific case study. |
| 4–6 | Sound understanding of feedback mechanisms. Includes a relevant case study with clear identification of the feedback loop and the resulting shift in equilibrium. Some analysis of consequences provided. |
| 7–9 | Substantial understanding of the relationship between positive feedback and tipping points. Effective use of a detailed case study. Thorough evaluation of the ecological and/or social consequences. Explicit judgments regarding the stability and irreversibility of the system. |

parts.content: Evaluate the effectiveness of the three-level model of pollution management (Replace, Regulate, Restore) when applied to the issue of plastic waste in urban environments.
parts.markscheme:
### Understanding Concepts and Terminology
- Level 1: Altering human activity (Replace) - e.g., changing consumer behavior, bans on single-use plastics <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Level 2: Controlling release (Regulate) - e.g., legislation, recycling infrastructure, waste management taxes <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Level 3: Clean-up and restoration (Restore) - e.g., ocean clean-ups, landfill remediation, removing microplastics from water <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Sustainability and long-term vs. short-term solutions <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Breadth in Addressing and Linking
- Level 1 effectiveness: Most sustainable, addresses root causes, but requires significant cultural shift/political will <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Level 2 effectiveness: Practical for immediate reductions, generates revenue for infrastructure, but can be bypassed or poorly enforced <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Level 3 effectiveness: Necessary for legacy pollution, but often expensive, inefficient, and does not stop the source <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Examples
- Specific mentions such as plastic bag bans in specific countries, the Great Pacific Garbage Patch clean-up efforts, or municipal recycling schemes <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Balanced Analysis
- Discussion of how the most effective approach usually requires a combination of all three levels (e.g., Level 1 is hardest to implement but most effective; Level 3 is easiest to visualize but least effective long-term) <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Conclusion
- A clear judgment supported by the preceding arguments. 
- Example: 'While clean-up efforts (Level 3) are highly publicized, they are ultimately futile without robust Level 1 and 2 interventions to stop the influx of plastic into the system.' <marks>1 mark</marks>

<marks>9 marks max</marks>

parts.content: Explain how the physical and chemical properties of Persistent Organic Pollutants (POPs) contribute to their biomagnification in arctic food webs.
parts.markscheme:
### Properties of POPs
- Chemical stability/persistence: POPs do not break down easily in the environment <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Solubility: They are lipid-soluble (fat-soluble) rather than water-soluble <marks>1 mark</marks>
- High molecular weight prevents easy excretion from the body <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Transport Mechanism
- Long-range atmospheric transport (the 'grasshopper effect'): POPs volatilize in warm regions and condense in colder, polar regions <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Biomagnification Process
- Bioaccumulation: Pollutants build up within the tissues of a single organism over its lifetime <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Biomagnification: Concentration of the pollutant increases as it moves up through successive trophic levels <marks>1 mark</marks>
$$
\text{Plankton} \rightarrow \text{Fish} \rightarrow \text{Seal} \rightarrow \text{Polar Bear (Highest concentration)}
$$

### Impacts and Examples
- Specific mention of a pollutant (e.g., DDT, PCBs, or Dioxins) and its impact on apex predators (e.g., thinning eggshells, immune suppression, or reproductive failure) <marks>1 mark</marks>

<marks>7 marks max</marks>

parts.content: Compare point source pollution and non-point source pollution, providing one specific example of each.
parts.markscheme:
### Point Source Pollution
- Definition: Pollution released from a single, clearly identifiable site or pipe <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Characteristic: Usually easier to monitor, regulate, and control legally as the culprit is known <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Example: A specific factory chimney emitting smoke or a sewage pipe discharging into a river <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Non-point Source Pollution
- Definition: Pollution released from numerous, widely dispersed origins (diffuse) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Characteristic: Difficult to attribute to one source; requires regional or international management strategies <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Example: Runoff of nitrates from agricultural fields or exhaust fumes from thousands of individual cars in a city <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award 4 marks max. To achieve full marks, both types must be defined/characterized and both must have a valid example.</note>

<marks>4 marks max</marks>

parts.content: Identify **four** distinct categories of ecosystem services, as defined by the Millennium Ecosystem Assessment, that provide 'natural income' for human societies.
parts.markscheme:
- Supporting services (e.g., primary production, soil formation, nutrient cycling) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Provisioning services (e.g., food, fresh water, timber, fiber, fuel) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Regulating services (e.g., climate regulation, flood regulation, water purification, pollination) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Outline the sequence of procedures required to conduct a comprehensive Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) for a proposed urban development project.
parts.markscheme:
- **Screening**: Determine if the project requires an EIA based on local legislation/scale of impact. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Scoping**: Identify the key environmental issues and requirements that need to be addressed. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Baseline Study**: Conduct a detailed inventory of the existing environment (biotic and abiotic factors) before development starts. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Impact Prediction**: Use models or expert judgment to estimate the potential scale and duration of environmental changes. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Mitigation Planning**: Propose specific measures to avoid, reduce, or compensate for identified negative impacts. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Environmental Statement (Production)**: Compile all findings and mitigation strategies into a formal report for public and government review. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Monitoring and Auditing**: Establish a program to track actual impacts during and after construction to ensure compliance and effectiveness of mitigation. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each distinct procedural stage described, up to a maximum of <marks>7 marks</marks>.</note>

parts.content: To what extent can the **Ecological Footprint (EF)** be considered a more effective model for measuring the sustainability of a human population than a **Natural Capital** assessment?
parts.markscheme:
_Answers may include_:

*   **Understanding of concepts**: Ecological Footprint (the area of land and water required to sustainably provide all resources at the rate at which they are being consumed and to assimilate all waste); Natural Capital (the stock of natural resources that produces a sustainable yield of goods and services).
*   **Breadth of comparison**: EF as a single indicator of consumption vs. Natural Capital as a measurement of available stocks; national vs. global scales; use of biocapacity; ecological overshoot; non-renewable vs. renewable capital.
*   **Examples**: Comparison of EF between MEDCs and LEDCs; specific examples of natural capital (e.g., fossil fuels vs. timber); the role of technological advancement in changing EF; local depletion of natural capital despite a stable global EF.
*   **Balanced analysis**: 
    *   **Strengths of EF**: Simple, easy to communicate/compare between countries, highlights global inequality, clearly demonstrates overshoot (where consumption exceeds regeneration).
    *   **Weaknesses of EF**: Ignores many forms of pollution (e.g., toxics, plastics), assumes land is used for only one purpose, does not distinguish between sustainable and unsustainable land management within the footprint.
    *   **Strengths of Natural Capital**: More comprehensive for local resource management, accounts for both biotic and abiotic (non-renewable) stocks, useful for economic valuation/GDP greening.
    *   **Weaknesses of Natural Capital**: Harder to quantify in a single metric, difficult to account for the mobility of resources (trade).
*   **Conclusion**: A conclusion that is supported by the analysis provided. _e.g._, "While the Ecological Footprint is a superior tool for raising public awareness and showing global imbalances, Natural Capital assessments provide the detailed data necessary for local policy-making and managing specific resource stocks sustainably."

parts.content: Using examples, outline the differences between direct and indirect measures used to assess water quality.
parts.markscheme:
### Direct Measures

Direct measures of pollution record the actual amounts of the pollutant in an aquatic system. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Example: measuring the concentration of DDT in a water sample. <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Indirect Measures

Indirect measures record the changes in biotic and abiotic factors caused by the pollutant. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Example: the measurement of the biochemical oxygen demand in water to check for organic pollution. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain the role of abiotic limiting factors in determining the global distribution of terrestrial biomes.
parts.markscheme:
- **Insolation/Solar radiation**: Varies with latitude; higher intensity at the equator drives higher temperatures and rates of photosynthesis/primary productivity. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Temperature**: Determines the length of the growing season and metabolic rates of organisms; extreme cold (tundra) or heat (deserts) limits species diversity. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Precipitation**: Total annual rainfall and its seasonal distribution (e.g., tropical rainforest vs. savanna) determine the available moisture for plant growth. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **P/E Ratio (Precipitation to Evaporation)**: If P/E is >1, water is plentiful (forests); if P/E is <1, evaporation exceeds rainfall, leading to water stress (deserts/grasslands). <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Do not credit biotic factors like competition or predation, as the question specifically asks for abiotic limiting factors in the context of biome distribution.</note>

parts.content: Distinguish between the processes of zonation and succession, using named examples to illustrate your answer.
parts.markscheme:
### Differences between Zonation and Succession

- **Definition of Zonation**: Spatial change in the community structure along an environmental gradient. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Definition of Succession**: Temporal change in the community structure over time at a single location. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Driving Factors**: 
  - Zonation is primarily driven by abiotic gradients (e.g., altitude, salinity, tidal exposure). <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Succession is driven by biotic interactions and the modification of the environment by the organisms themselves (e.g., soil formation). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Example of Zonation**: e.g., Different seaweed species arranged in horizontal bands on a rocky shore due to varying tolerance to desiccation. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Example of Succession**: e.g., The progression from bare rock to lichen, then moss, grasses, shrubs, and finally oak forest (lithosere). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Reversibility/Stability**: Zonation is usually a permanent feature of the landscape as long as the gradient exists, while succession moves toward a dynamic equilibrium (climax community). <marks>1 mark</marks>

<examTip>Students must clearly state which process is spatial and which is temporal to gain full marks for the distinction.</examTip>

parts.content: Evaluate the changes in ecosystem energetics and diversity that occur as a community develops from a pioneer stage to a climax community.
parts.markscheme:
_Answers may include_:

*   **Gross Primary Productivity (GPP)**: Low in pioneer stages due to low leaf area/biomass; increases significantly through intermediate stages as vegetation cover increases; may stabilize or slightly decline in the climax stage as the system reaches maturity.
*   **Net Primary Productivity (NPP)**: High in early/intermediate stages as plants invest energy in rapid growth; in the climax community, NPP approaches zero because GPP is balanced by high community respiration ($R$) ($P/R$ ratio approaches 1).
*   **Biomass**: Increases steadily throughout succession, reaching its maximum at the climax stage where complex vertical stratification (e.g., canopy layers) is present.
*   **Diversity**: 
    - **Species richness**: Usually increases through the seral stages.
    - **Diversity indices**: Low in pioneer stages (few hardy species); peaks in late-intermediate stages; may slightly decrease in the final climax stage as dominant species outcompete others.
*   **Nutrient Cycling**: Pioneer stages have open cycles with high nutrient loss; climax communities have closed, efficient cycles with nutrients stored primarily in the biomass rather than the soil.
*   **Food Web Complexity**: Transitions from simple linear food chains (pioneer) to complex, branched food webs with many trophic levels and specialized niches (climax), leading to increased stability.
*   **Conclusion**: Succession represents a shift from $r$-strategists (pioneers) to $K$-strategists (climax), where the ecosystem moves from a state of high energy accumulation to a state of high maintenance and complexity.

<note>Award 7-9 marks if the answer provides a balanced evaluation of both energetics (GPP, NPP, R) and diversity/complexity with a clear progression through seral stages.</note>

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/6ffc1b26-09ef-4694-87a5-e21395a40b76)

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 4: Water > 4.4 Water pollution > 4.4C Eutrophication
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 4.4C Eutrophication
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The inverse relationship between BOD and dissolved oxygen is a fundamental concept in the process of eutrophication and organic pollution impacts on aquatic ecosystems.

parts.content: Describe the relationship shown in the graph.
parts.markscheme:
- As BOD decreases, dissolved oxygen increases <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The relationship is inverse or negative <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain the significance of this relationship for aquatic ecosystems.
parts.markscheme:
- High BOD indicates high levels of organic pollution <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Low dissolved oxygen can lead to hypoxic conditions and fish kills <marks>1 mark</marks>

labeledSubSubTopics.title: 4.4B Water quality
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks for methods to assess water quality in a river system, which directly corresponds to the 'Water quality' sub-topic that covers biological (e.g., biotic indices), chemical (e.g., dissolved oxygen, pH), and physical (e.g., turbidity) indicators of water health.

parts.content: Describe two ways in which water quality can be assessed in a river system.
parts.markscheme:
#### Method #1

Chemical analysis: Measure parameters like ${pH}$, dissolved oxygen, nutrients

Dissolved oxygen levels indicate the amount of oxygen available for aquatic life

pH measures acidity/alkalinity which affects biological processes

Nutrient levels like nitrates and phosphates can lead to eutrophication

<marks>2 marks</marks>

#### Method #2

Biological indicators: Use presence/absence of certain species to indicate water quality

- Some organisms are sensitive to pollution, their absence suggests poor water quality
- Other organisms are tolerant of pollution, their presence indicates contamination

<marks>2 marks</marks>
